title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: Lecture 37
text:
For a binary outcome (0/1, No/Yes), we replace normal-errors linear regression with a binomial model:
\[\text{logit}(p) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x,\ \ \ p = P(Y=1|X=x)\]
Fit in R with:

glm(y ~ x, family = binomial, data = ...)

Main prediction target is probability p, then classify using a cutoff (often 0.5 unless context suggests otherwise).


By the end of this lecture, you should be able to:

explain why logistic regression is used for binary outcomes;
fit and interpret a simple logistic regression model with glm(..., family = binomial);
interpret the slope as a change in log-odds (and direction of odds change);
compute and interpret a confidence interval for the logistic slope;
generate predicted probabilities and classify outcomes using a cutoff;
compare training and test accuracy and interpret out-of-sample classification performance.








Code
if (!requireNamespace("tidyverse", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("tidyverse", repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}

library(tidyverse)

set.seed(3601)
n <- 160
pass_dat <- tibble(
  study_hours = runif(n, 0, 15)
) |>
  mutate(
    p_pass = plogis(-2.4 + 0.38 * study_hours),
    pass = rbinom(n(), 1, p_pass)
  )






Code
set.seed(3602)
idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(pass_dat)), size = 0.8 * nrow(pass_dat))
pass_train <- pass_dat[idx, ]
pass_test  <- pass_dat[-idx, ]

fit_pass <- glm(pass ~ study_hours, family = binomial, data = pass_train)
summary(fit_pass)



Call:
glm(formula = pass ~ study_hours, family = binomial, data = pass_train)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept) -2.91993    0.57498  -5.078 3.81e-07 ***
study_hours  0.44040    0.07299   6.034 1.60e-09 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 172.92  on 127  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 109.15  on 126  degrees of freedom
AIC: 113.15

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5







Code
confint(fit_pass, "study_hours")


    2.5 %    97.5 % 
0.3092083 0.5978433 


If the CI is entirely above 0, more study hours are associated with higher odds of passing.





Code
pass_train <- pass_train |>
  mutate(
    p_hat = predict(fit_pass, newdata = pass_train, type = "response"),
    y_hat_50 = if_else(p_hat >= 0.5, 1, 0)
  )

pass_test <- pass_test |>
  mutate(
    p_hat = predict(fit_pass, newdata = pass_test, type = "response"),
    y_hat_50 = if_else(p_hat >= 0.5, 1, 0)
  )







Code
acc <- function(y, yhat) mean(y == yhat)

tibble(
  set = c("train", "test"),
  accuracy = c(
    acc(pass_train$pass, pass_train$y_hat_50),
    acc(pass_test$pass, pass_test$y_hat_50)
  )
)


# A tibble: 2 × 2
  set   accuracy
  <chr>    <dbl>
1 train    0.844
2 test     0.844


Training accuracy can be optimistic; test accuracy better represents performance on new data.





Code
new_students <- tibble(study_hours = c(4, 8, 12))
p_new <- predict(fit_pass, newdata = new_students, type = "response")
tibble(study_hours = new_students$study_hours, predicted_probability_pass = p_new)


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  study_hours predicted_probability_pass
        <dbl>                      <dbl>
1           4                      0.239
2           8                      0.646
3          12                      0.914




Code
cutoff_demo <- tibble(
  cutoff = c(0.3, 0.5, 0.7),
  predicted_class_for_p0_62 = if_else(0.62 >= cutoff, 1, 0)
)
cutoff_demo


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  cutoff predicted_class_for_p0_62
   <dbl>                     <dbl>
1    0.3                         1
2    0.5                         1
3    0.7                         0









Code
set.seed(3603)
bloom_dat <- tibble(
  dose = runif(140, 0, 12)
) |>
  mutate(
    p_bloom = plogis(-1.9 + 0.42 * dose),
    bloom = rbinom(n(), 1, p_bloom)
  )

idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(bloom_dat)), size = 0.8 * nrow(bloom_dat))
bloom_train <- bloom_dat[idx, ]
bloom_test  <- bloom_dat[-idx, ]

fit_bloom <- glm(bloom ~ dose, family = binomial, data = bloom_train)
summary(fit_bloom)



Call:
glm(formula = bloom ~ dose, family = binomial, data = bloom_train)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept) -2.28728    0.49638  -4.608 4.07e-06 ***
dose         0.50458    0.09239   5.462 4.72e-08 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 152.97  on 111  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 101.94  on 110  degrees of freedom
AIC: 105.94

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5


Code
confint(fit_bloom, "dose")


    2.5 %    97.5 % 
0.3392949 0.7051015 


Code
bloom_test <- bloom_test |>
  mutate(
    p_hat = predict(fit_bloom, newdata = bloom_test, type = "response"),
    y_hat_50 = if_else(p_hat >= 0.5, 1, 0)
  )

tibble(test_accuracy = mean(bloom_test$bloom == bloom_test$y_hat_50))


# A tibble: 1 × 1
  test_accuracy
          <dbl>
1          0.75








Code
set.seed(3604)
bp_dat <- tibble(
  exercise_min = runif(170, 0, 70)
) |>
  mutate(
    p_hyp = plogis(0.9 - 0.045 * exercise_min),
    hypertension = rbinom(n(), 1, p_hyp)
  )

idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(bp_dat)), size = 0.8 * nrow(bp_dat))
bp_train <- bp_dat[idx, ]
bp_test  <- bp_dat[-idx, ]

fit_bp <- glm(hypertension ~ exercise_min, family = binomial, data = bp_train)
summary(fit_bp)



Call:
glm(formula = hypertension ~ exercise_min, family = binomial, 
    data = bp_train)

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)   0.99507    0.36133   2.754  0.00589 ** 
exercise_min -0.04804    0.01033  -4.651  3.3e-06 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 178.89  on 135  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 152.25  on 134  degrees of freedom
AIC: 156.25

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4


Code
confint(fit_bp, "exercise_min")


      2.5 %      97.5 % 
-0.06947402 -0.02873974 


Code
bp_test <- bp_test |>
  mutate(
    p_hat = predict(fit_bp, newdata = bp_test, type = "response"),
    y_hat_50 = if_else(p_hat >= 0.5, 1, 0)
  )

tibble(
  test_accuracy = mean(bp_test$hypertension == bp_test$y_hat_50),
  p_at_20min = predict(fit_bp, newdata = tibble(exercise_min = 20), type = "response"),
  p_at_50min = predict(fit_bp, newdata = tibble(exercise_min = 50), type = "response")
)


# A tibble: 1 × 3
  test_accuracy p_at_20min p_at_50min
          <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl>
1         0.794      0.509      0.197







Logistic regression models probability for a binary outcome via the logit link.
Use training data to fit the model; report test accuracy for generalization.
Slope CI tells whether the log-odds effect is plausibly positive, negative, or near zero.
Probability prediction comes first; class prediction depends on chosen cutoff.

title: Lecture 37
Lecture 37
text:

# A tibble: 3 × 2

        <dbl>                      <dbl>
1           4                      0.239
2           8                      0.646
3          12                      0.914




Code

  cutoff = c(0.3, 0.5, 0.7),
  predicted_class_for_p0_62 = if_else(0.62 >= cutoff, 1, 0)
)
cutoff_demo


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  cutoff predicted_class_for_p0_62
   <dbl>                     <dbl>
1    0.3                         1
2    0.5                         1
3    0.7                         0

title: Lecture 37
text:
Code
set.seed(3603)
bloom_dat <- tibble(
  dose = runif(140, 0, 12)
) |>
  mutate(
    p_bloom = plogis(-1.9 + 0.42 * dose),
    bloom = rbinom(n(), 1, p_bloom)
  )

idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(bloom_dat)), size = 0.8 * nrow(bloom_dat))
bloom_train <- bloom_dat[idx, ]
bloom_test  <- bloom_dat[-idx, ]

fit_bloom <- glm(bloom ~ dose, family = binomial, data = bloom_train)
summary(fit_bloom)

Coefficients:

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

title: Lecture 37
text:
Logistic regression models probability for a binary outcome via the logit link.
Use training data to fit the model; report test accuracy for generalization.
Slope CI tells whether the log-odds effect is plausibly positive, negative, or near zero.
Probability prediction comes first; class prediction depends on chosen cutoff.
